text: This is disgusting. I respected you and would've called you a friend at one point. But you had to go ahead and make fun of something that was absolutely tragic for someone's family. This man could've easily killed someone in that home, and you ridicule the entire situation.
author: @kaytethefloof

text: Well, the memeability of this would've been very different and possibly nonexistent had he actually taken someone's life.  I'm glad it didn't end that way.  But the truth is, the world is getting massive entertainment out of this right now simply because of how absurd and unhinged it is.  The underlying context and reality of the situation is not part of the joke and not what I'm focused on.  And it's disappointing to have viewers who consider themselves "friends" who suddenly turn on me. Most regular viewers know I don't hold punches when it comes to using controversial source material or making edgy jokes.  How many 9/11 jokes have I made? Quite a few. My dad was involved in 9/11 but managed to escape.  Making fun of tragedies can be a coping mechanism (though not everyone agrees and that's okay).  Also, I put disclaimers on my videos for a reason. Just because I omitted the intro disclaimer on this one, you think I am trying to be offensive?  Like, if you've watched me for years and years, how could this suddenly be the thing that turns you off?  The backlash baffles me.

text: I'm not really sure it's ok to make light of this guy's mental issues man... you're better than this bro.

text: FYI some of my favorite sources are people who have mental issues / are loud and eccentric, such as Tourettes Guy, Winnebago Man, Steve Ballmer, so somebody acting like a lunatic is right up my alley.  This one isn't an "act" of course, so I understand your position here.  Keep in mind, I too have mental issues which I don't disclose.  Maybe not as bad as this person, but to assume I'm fully sane or a normal functioning member of society would be naive 🤪
author: @cs188

text:
@cs188  Yeah but this seems hateful. You don't have to be neurotypical to kinda be shitty towards people with mental issues. The victim in this case would probably also discourage doing this. I'm assuming that you aren't intentionally being awful, but this rubs me the wrong way heavily. People with mental issues get ostracized and made fun of way too much to the point it's straight up normalized and people don't realize how shitty they're being. Please do better.
EDIT: The comments here are also just reeking of ableism all around... I might have to stop watching your content since I'm not liking your community. at. all.
author: @polocatfan

text: @polocatfan  Dude, everybody gets made fun of. You're not special. Stop taking it so personally and get off the internet.
author: @CatterCattington

text: I can respect your decision, but to say I'm hateful towards a specific group of people would be incorrect.  I make fun of ANY and ALL people, no matter their condition.  I don't know their backstories.  I have always done this, and I don't know why it surprises you if you're a long-time viewer of this channel.  I even make fun of myself here with self-YTPs.  If I ever go batshit crazy like this, I absolutely want to be memed to hell.  I'm on an endless crusade to teach people to lighten up, and that's part of the reason I do what I do here.
author: @cs188

text: @polocatfan  I don't know what you think ableism is, but this isn't it. This is just making fun of him for the sake of it, not because he is disabled. It's not making fun of disabled people in general. It's just using a source and pooping the shit out of it. Don't look so deep into it.
author: @Yabuturtle

text: @cs188  I'm sorry but you are absolutely being hateful here. And the fact you didn't even respond to the fact that people are being massively ableist in the comments section here isn't exactly making me take you in good faith. Please do better.
author: @polocatfan

text: Well, you haven't given any further explanation as to why you think this edit is "hateful", you only rejected my statement. I don't agree.  Also when it comes to my viewers comments, I don't speak for my viewers, so I have no opinion on whether or not they're being "ableist". Ableism is discrimination from what I understand.  I don't discriminate.  If my viewers do, then maybe you really just don't like my community.  And hey, that's fine, they sometimes aren't the best of the bunch.  It sounds like you'd be right at home in DaThings' community, who specifically focus on non-discriminatory and inclusive practices.
author: @cs188

text: Also, I'm getting the impression you think I am normal and not mentally disabled. I am ADHD and Autistic, maybe also Bipolar.  I know exactly what it's like to have sudden uncontrollable feelings of rage, just like this guy.  I could see myself in his shoes if my life went differently.  So I do consider it making fun of my own kind, to a degree.

author: @xXx_Regulus_xXx

text:
​​ @xXx_Regulus_xXx So to be clear, that hypothetical scenario only happens in  extremely rare cases and also been proven several times through many test trials that having unarmed medical response teams answering calls for people with psychosis not only works, but actually deescalates the situation compared to say, oh I don't know. sending a whole team with guns in to deal with one individual.

The only reason why we don't have those unarm response teams isn't because those individuals are dangerous which is btw, a gross generalization of those people, but because there isn't any money in it compared to cops. That's basically it.
author: @DeadHandtheSurvivor

text: @DeadHandtheSurvivor  if you'll notice I wasn't generalizing, I was satirizing the idea of sending someone after a person who's in fight or flight mode, isn't in touch with reality, and has an adversarial relationship with authority. It's unethical to send someone to care for or subdue a person like that (who might be armed) without the means to defend themselves, just taking it on faith that either everyone experiencing psychosis is a lil bean just waiting for words of encouragement or that it's morally okay to feed social workers to crazy people as long as it only happens sometimes. I doubt you've ever dealt with this issue in reality, you just think it sounds nicer if there are no icky guns involved.
author: @xXx_Regulus_xXx

text:
​​ @xXx_Regulus_xXx But what you are describing rarely, if ever, happens, that is the point.

It is very rare in many instances of people having these episodes, of actually lashing out in violence towards individuals, and if it does, usually is because they are seeing people in uniforms wearing bullet proof suits and are armed to the teeth.

Once again, it has been proven several times especially in Europe, that when you respond to these kind of calls unarmed, and been actually trained to deal with this sort of cases, that these scenarios can actually be deescalated because, you know, they've been trained to deal with this.

And before you say anything, the only reason why this particular individual lashed out in violence was because the person who was responding to him did not realize he was going through an episode, and thought he was just some person being disrespectful.

We need to stop thinking just because somebody is capable of committing violence during an episode means they're going to do it. It is just simply not true a huge percentage of time

Also, if someone was trained to deal with these kind of cases, they wouldn't straight out say who they are. They would just treat them like a friend. Because that's what they're told to do, your scenario is based on something that these professionals would never do. Like, come on man.
author: @DeadHandtheSurvivor

text:
@DeadHandtheSurvivor  I already addressed the "rarity" angle. to reiterate, if you're ok with feeding well meaning people to crazy people as long as it's under some arbitrary threshold of occurrences you're immoral. they can use deescalation tactics without being left defenseless for these supposedly rare cases where only one party gets to walk away.
I don't weigh "proven in Europe" very heavily, they have an abysmal track record on integration and protection of vulnerable citizens.
author: @xXx_Regulus_xXx

text:
​ @xXx_Regulus_xXx for the last time, what do you think you're doing, when these people see you with a weapon, why is this so difficult to understand?

Also, you think these people don't understand the risk they take? But they don't care cause they want to help these people, that's the whole point of why they took the job.

I can't make this any more crystal clear.
author: @DeadHandtheSurvivor

text: ​​ @DeadHandtheSurvivor Not meaning to add more fuel to the fire, but I think carrying something akin to a taser would be okay. It's not lethal, easily concealed and meant for self defense. The responders shouldn't go out sporting assault rifles or even pistols but I don't think they should be defenseless neither.
author: @LarryTheTugaGamer1511

text:
@xXx_Regulus_xXx  I'm not sure if you understand what mental health workers actually do, or what kind of training they get. 

I'm no expert, either, but I have a couple of family members who work in related fields. It's not really about assuming someone is harmless or just giving them "words of encouragement." 

More like figuring out what their actual thought process is, so you can give them instructions they're more likely to comply with. Obviously, this will typically involve convincing the person to trust you, but if you're assuming they're ignorant and naive just because their tone of voice sounds friendly, that probably says more about your own level of knowledge on the subject than theirs. I believe most people who would even have this job would probably know more about predicting human behavior than the average cop does. 

Ethically speaking, why are you pretending that the choice to bring a weapon into a situation is some neutral, risk-free thing that has no influence on what else will happen? I don't think anyone needs a psychology degree to know that most people, sane or not, react to seeing a weapon by becoming less calm than they were before, especially if the weapon is already in someone's hand, or know that fear is one of the more common motivations for aggression. If you know someone's already paranoid, agitated, and "adversarial to authority," how is an authority figure brandishing a weapon at them likely to affect their state of mind? 

In a "welfare check" type of situation, we're talking about a person who hasn't actually done anything wrong. They were just being weird in a way that made someone else nervous. If your "means of defending yourself" ends up being the reason you even need to defend yourself, meaning it's the reason someone got hurt instead of no one getting hurt, you don't think that's an ethical issue in its own way?
author: @macdeus2601

text: @DeadHandtheSurvivor  I don't misunderstand your position, I disagree with it and I consider you immoral for holding it. Understanding that a job done the European way comes with completely avoidable risks and declaring some number of workers as victims of fate instead of equipping them to survive their job is as idiotic as not using a seatbelt. You can rephrase your barbaric, wrong-headed point again if you're so inclined but I'm not going to capitulate.
author: @xXx_Regulus_xXx

text: @macdeus2601  I addressed these points already. No, really, read all of my comments from beginning to end. You're arguing with a figment of your own imagination. You're speaking about the situation like I'm asking people to act like John Wick. I'm obviously not. I just prefer people who are willing to put themselves in harm's way get to go home at the end of their shift. There is absolutely nothing immoral about a lawful citizen acting in good faith having the means to survive if a situation escalates to violence, and it's slavish and wrong to think otherwise.
author: @xXx_Regulus_xXx

text:
@LarryTheTugaGamer1511 Thank you. I'm not suggesting anyone barge into a psychotic person's home waving around the Turbolaser Facemelter 9000, I'm saying Tina the Therapist should have discrete but effective protection when she makes a wellness check at Criminally Insane Craig's dark dingy apartment. I'd be more inclined to say she should have live rounds because some of the rare cases (which is what this convo is about, the edge cases) can resist a taser, but it'd probably be enough depending on the person's specific body a lot of the time.
But certain kinds of people hear about any weapon carried as insurance and go into a panic spiral.
author: @xXx_Regulus_xXx

text:
@xXx_Regulus_xXx  Well, you seem to be missing the other person's point, as well as mine. 

You're acting like a situation "escalating to violence" cannot be a result of someone deciding to bring a "means of self-defense" but it most certainly can be. 

Either that, or you're arguing the person who brought a weapon has no responsibility for that consequence of their decision--or possibly you're suggesting there's no ethical value in preventing an avoidable death under these circumstances. 

I don't agree with either of those ideas, and in my experience people who advocate expanding non-police, unarmed psychiatric field interventions would strongly disagree with them. 

One of their main concerns seems to be the risk of a situation that could have ended peacefully leading to a mentally ill person getting injured or killed by a police officer because they freaked out about the police officer being there, and/or being armed. 

As the other person here was saying, that scenario actually has a higher likelihood of happening than a scenario where the mentally ill person attacks an unarmed health worker. 

So I suppose in a sense, it boils down to a question of what your priorities are. You take a risk either way.
author: @macdeus2601

text: ​ @DeadHandtheSurvivor  many cops do undergo CIT training to deal with this. However, I wouldn't want to be without defense in case of worst case scenario.
author: @dreyri2736

text: ​​ @DeadHandtheSurvivor  just because it is rare does not mean that we should therefore not give responders guns to defend themselves just in case. To say that a normal cop is "armed to the teeth" because he has a vest and a pistol for defense is silly. You might as well say that we need to teach people to just not assume that a cop is going to shoot you just because he has a pistol. I mean, you are already on board with drilling into people that just because some sketchy person is kicking your door and shouting blood-curdling screams at you does not mean that they are actually going to do anything at all.
author: @dreyri2736

text: ​@LarryTheTugaGamer1511 there are many cases of tasers not actually doing anything, especially when a person is extremely angry and typically when they realize that the taser doesn't work on them, they start getting cockier.
author: @dreyri2736

text:
​​ @macdeus2601  it isn't a question of managing to calm a person down. It's a question of self-defense. You don't even need to be a cop to realize how dangerous their line of work can be. There are countless body-cam footages available for free on youtube that show them dealing with surprise life or death situations. These guys are not trained to disregard their own safety and life. I doubt psychiatric professionals are either. 

Think about this seriously: do you really think that a single gun, or a person in uniform, can really cause so much of a difference that it changes a situation into a fatal one? How do you even test that? You'd have to somehow reproduce the same scenario again where you eliminate the gun variable, but that is not possible in an ethical manner I believe.

I'd wager that a lot of the time the police officer is scary because he is seen as the executive of the law and has the power to throw you in jail. But this is a legal procedure and our liberal societies are not too keen on giving exceptions to the law, though some people at the same time no longer like the whole "equality before the law" thing, it seems.
author: @dreyri2736
